title: Argument of printf should be a format string
description: Argument of "printf" should be a format stringAnalyze your codeconsistency-conventionalmaintainabilityCode Smellbad-practiceWhy is this an issue?More InfoIt is a security vulnerability to callprintfwith a unique string argument that is not a string literal. Indeed, if this argument comes from a user input, this user can:make the program crash by executing code equivalent to:printf("%s%s%s%s%s%s%s%s")view the stack or memory at any location by executing code equivalent to:printf("%08x %08x %08x %08x %08x\n")Starting with C++23,std::printshould be preferred: its arguments are validated at compile-time, making it more secure.Noncompliant code examplevoid f(char* userInput) { printf(userInput); // Noncompliant }Compliant solutionvoid f(char* userInput) { printf("%s", userInput); // Compliant }
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant: printf(userInput);                    // Security risk - user input as format string
printf(variable);                     // Variable used directly as format string
printf(buffer);                       // Buffer content used as format string
printf(argv[1]);                      // Command line arg used as format string
printf(getMessage());                 // Function return used as format string
compliant: printf("%s", userInput);              // User input as argument with format specifier
printf("Hello World");                // String literal as format string
printf("%s %d", name, age);           // Format specifiers with arguments
printf("%s", variable);               // Variable as argument, not format string
puts(userInput);                      // Alternative safe function for simple output

checks.message: Printf called with variable format string. Use string literal as format string and pass variables as arguments to prevent format string attacks.
